text: Following robotic docking, the surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon to the left side of the abdomen, providing clear exposure of the pelvic region for the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon performs a five-centimeter peritoneal incision at the Douglas pouch, using robotic instruments to access the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon dissects the rectovaginal space, extending the dissection up to the perineal body, revealing the anatomical separation between the rectum and vagina.

The surgeon introduces a polypropylene mesh into the abdominal cavity, preparing for its placement in the rectopexy procedure.

Two radiopaque clips are visible on the distal edge of the previously placed mesh in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon sutures the polypropylene mesh over the ventral rectum using six TRIO PDS sutures, securing the mesh in place as part of the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon preserves the most proximal sutures for further fixation at the proximal vagina, ensuring secure attachment of the mesh in the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon performs a small peritoneal incision, approximately three centimeters, at the level of the sacral promontory. The periosteum is carefully freed, avoiding damage to the autonomic nerve fibers and lateral and utero sacral ligaments. A retroperitoneal tunnel is accurately created from the pre-promontory incision to the pelvic incision.

The surgeon pulls the proximal end of the polypropylene mesh upward, positioning it at the sacral promontory within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon applies gentle tension to the mesh to prevent future rectal interception and rectocele, securing it at the sacral promontory with two OPDS stitches.

Two clips are placed on the mesh and an additional clip on the periosteum to facilitate future radiographic checks for potential mesh detachment.

The surgeon trims the redundant portion of the mesh, ensuring it fits appropriately within the surgical field.
